text: Tangible Outcomes visual strategy is used for physical services (home services, professional services, automotive repair, medical procedures). Core principle: show sensory, measurable results of the service rather than the process. Visual anchors: gleaming clean surfaces, before/after contrast implied through lighting differences, skilled hands performing precise work, transformed living spaces with natural lighting. Lifestyle vibe: confident, relieved, satisfied homeowner or professional in a natural setting. Camera: 50mm prime lens, f/2.8 shallow depth of field, subject in sharp focus. Subjects should NOT look at camera. Natural light preferred, warm tones.

Visualizing Virtual Value strategy is used for experience-mediated services accessed through apps or platforms — travel booking, healthcare, food delivery, education, fitness. Core principle: prioritize the real-world physical environment and human experience over the digital interface. A person enjoying the result of using the service (at a destination, feeling healthy, eating a meal) carries more visual weight than the app screen. Device or screen is optional — if shown, it must not dominate the composition. Camera: 35mm prime lens, natural environmental lighting, lifestyle photography tone. Composition: person naturally engaged in the experience. No posed shots looking at camera.

Functional Clarity strategy is used for interface-as-product tools — productivity software, SaaS dashboards, design tools, developer platforms, analytics tools, CRM. Core principle: the UI *is* the product, so show it with editorial dignity. Composition: symmetric, poster-like layout with the interface as visual hero. Partial, readable UI elements (buttons, data ribbons, input fields) visible at slight natural angle — never perfectly flat. Clean editorial background, restrained color palette. Strictly NO humans, hands, or faces in frame. AGI/Digital Poster aesthetic: product stands alone. Depth: shallow bokeh with sharp UI elements in focus.
